narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands out above the typical student's 3.21. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 52%, tipping the decision toward rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by a few key factors from your profile that pulled in different directions. Your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st—well below the average 5.53—had the strongest push toward predicting failure, signaling to the model a notable dip in later academic performance compared to peers. On a positive note, your undergraduate GPA was the top factor working in your favor, strongly nudging the prediction toward success and showing your college-level excellence exceeds what's typical. Your low family income bracket also helped, as it contributed positively toward passing in the model's eyes, perhaps reflecting resilience common among students in similar situations. Your LSAT score of 26, lower than the average 36.70, added some concern by pushing toward failure, while your lower Year 1 decile of 2nd (versus the average 5.71) had a milder negative influence. Being enrolled full-time, like most students at 92.7%, barely factored in. Overall, your profile compares to typical applicants with stronger high school deciles and LSATs but shines in undergraduate grades and background resilience. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year academics through targeted prep, and reapplying with updated achievements that highlight your proven undergraduate success. You're clearly capable, and with these steps, you can shift the odds in your favor for future opportunities. Keep pushing—you've got this.
